title: Managing ventilation after draughtproofing
subtitle: Keeping air quality and moisture in balance once a home has been sealed
excerpt: Reducing a home's uncontrolled air infiltration from a typical 10 m3/h.m2 to below 5 m3/h.m2 cuts heating losses but raises the risk of condensation and elevated indoor CO2 if background ventilation is not maintained. Trickle vents, intermittent extract fans in wet rooms, and humidity-controlled vents provide the minimum controlled airflow the sealed envelope needs.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part F1 (2021) for equivalent area requirements in dwellings. BRE Digest 369 for condensation risk assessment. CIBSE AM10 for natural ventilation design principles. EST draughtproofing guidance 2024.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small adjustable slot ventilator fitted into a window frame or wall that provides continuous low-level background ventilation without creating a noticeable draught. Part F requires a minimum equivalent area per room.

Rapid ventilation by fully opening windows to remove high concentrations of pollutants, moisture, or CO2. Building Regulations Part F requires purge ventilation to be available in all habitable rooms.

The percentage of moisture the air holds relative to its maximum at a given temperature. Indoor air above 70 percent RH for prolonged periods raises the risk of condensation on cold surfaces and mould growth.

Condensation that forms within a building assembly, such as inside a wall or roof, when warm moist air meets a surface below dew point. It causes rot, mould, and loss of insulation performance without being visible at the surface.

content.text: Sealing a home reduces the uncontrolled air exchange that previously diluted moisture and CO2. This is intentional: the heat savings are real. But sealed buildings depend on controlled ventilation to replace what random draughts used to provide. Part F of Building Regulations sets minimum background ventilation requirements for this reason. Fitting
trickle vents
to windows and upgrading extract fans in kitchens and bathrooms is the standard response. The cost is modest, typically under 200 pounds, and the benefit is a drier, healthier home.

Part F (2021) requires background ventilators in each habitable room with a minimum equivalent area of 8,000 mm2 for a one-bedroom dwelling, rising to 10,000 mm2 for two bedrooms and above. Extract ventilation in kitchens must achieve 60 l/s (continuous) or 30 l/s (intermittent), and bathrooms 15 l/s continuous or 8 l/s intermittent. When replacing windows in an existing dwelling, the new frames must include trickle vents if the existing ones had them.
What you need

Trickle vents to fit existing window frames: slot-type vents that cut into the top rail of the outer frame, rated at 2,500 to 5,000 mm2 equivalent area each. Measure the frame rebate width before ordering.
Intermittent extract fan rated at 15 l/s minimum for bathroom use. A humidity-controlled fan is a good option: it ramps up when the air gets damp and drops back once the humidity falls.
A CO2 and humidity monitor (under 30 pounds) to check the ventilation is working. Read it before and after making changes.

Replacing or adding an extract fan in a bathroom or kitchen may require a Part P notified electrical installation if new wiring is needed. If you are extending a circuit into a zone 1 or zone 2 bathroom area, the work must be carried out or certified by a registered electrician. Replacing a like-for-like fan on an existing switched spur is a straightforward DIY task.
Fitting trickle vents to existing frames
Measure the top rail of the window frame for the available rebate width. Most trickle vent kits include a template. Mark the slot position so it centres on the rail width.

Cut the slot with a utility knife or oscillating multi-tool to the dimensions on the template. The slot typically passes through the outer frame and the internal lining. Work slowly to avoid splitting the frame.
Clip the outer cover and inner cover onto the frame either side of the slot. Check the vent opens and closes freely. The vent should be left open at all times except when
purge ventilation
by fully opening the window is being used.
Monitoring the result
After fitting trickle vents and upgrading the extract fans, check the bedroom
relative humidity

on a cold morning. In a well-ventilated room the RH should stay below 70 percent even on cold wet days. CO2 levels in occupied bedrooms overnight should fall below 1,500 ppm with the trickle vent open. If RH stays above 70 percent or CO2 above 1,500 ppm, the ventilation is still inadequate and additional trickle vents or a passive wall vent may be needed.
What done well looks like

All habitable rooms have at least one open trickle vent. The bathroom extract fan runs for 15 minutes after the light is switched off (overrun timer). RH in bedrooms stays below 65 percent overnight with the door closed and one occupant. No condensation forms on the glass of double-glazed units in cold weather. The house does not smell stuffy after several days without opening windows.
What done badly looks like

All draughts were sealed but no trickle vents were fitted, leaving the house with no background ventilation. Within two weeks, condensation appears on bedroom windows and mould begins to form on the external wall in the bedroom corner. The extract fan in the bathroom runs for 30 seconds and then trips off, which is not long enough to remove the moisture from a shower.
Interstitial condensation
develops behind the new draught-sealed window reveals where the vapour-impermeable seal has removed the drying pathway.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Condensation on windows despite trickle vents being open
attrs.cause: Bedroom door is kept closed overnight, trapping moisture from occupants' breath with no air exchange to the trickle vent
attrs.fix: Leave the bedroom door slightly ajar overnight to allow cross-ventilation from multiple trickle vents. A gap of 10 to 20 mm under the door is enough in most cases.

attrs.symptom: Noticeable cold draught from the trickle vent in winter
attrs.cause: The vent is positioned directly above a sitting area and cold air falls across the occupants
attrs.fix: Relocate the vent to a position above a radiator so the incoming cold air is immediately mixed with rising warm air. If relocation is not possible, fit a deflector cap on the inner cover to direct the incoming air upward toward the ceiling.

attrs.symptom: Bathroom RH stays above 80 percent after a shower for more than 30 minutes
attrs.cause: Extract fan is undersized or the overrun timer is set too short
attrs.fix: Check the fan airflow rating. An 8 l/s fan in a bathroom over 4 m2 is insufficient. Replace with a 15 l/s continuous or 30 l/s intermittent fan and set the overrun timer to at least 20 minutes.
